definition: Current yield curve steepening is primarily term-premium-driven (back end rising on supply/fiscal pressure) rather than growth-expectations-driven (front end falling on rate cut expectations). This 'bad steepening' carries qualitatively different implications for credit and equity markets than 'good steepening.'
evidence: ACM term premium swung from -50bp (2020) to +50-100bp (early 2026)
$2T+ annual Treasury issuance creates persistent supply pressure with no corresponding acceleration in potential output growth
Breakevens range-bound at 2.2-2.5%, not rising with real yields as good steepening would imply
2023 Q3 episode: 10Y rose 90bp driven by term premium, S&P fell ~10% with no material earnings decline—pure discount rate event
open_questions: How long can bad steepening persist before triggering recession or policy response?
What observable conditions would mark a transition from bad to good steepening?
Does the steepening trap pattern apply when the mechanism is bear-steepening rather than bull-steepening?
